preamble.summary: David's great hymn of praise to the LORD for His compassion, forgiveness, and faithful love. The psalm opens with a self-summons to bless God ('Bless the LORD, O my soul'), catalogs God's benefits — healing, redemption, satisfaction, renewal — then meditates on God's character as revealed to Moses: compassionate, slow to anger, abounding in faithful love. The central section (vv. 8-14) is the theological heart: God does not treat us as our sins deserve because He remembers that we are dust. The psalm closes with a cosmic call to praise, summoning angels, heavenly hosts, and all creation to bless the LORD.
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 103 is the chesed psalm — the word appears four times (vv. 4, 8, 11, 17), and the entire poem is an exposition of what chesed means in practice. The psalm quotes Exodus 34:6-7, the self-revelation of God to Moses after the golden calf disaster, and then extends it: 'He does not deal with us according to our sins' (v. 10). This is grace articulated before the word 'grace' acquired its Christian theological weight. The psalm's movement is striking: it begins with 'my soul' (individual), expands to Israel's history (national), then reaches to heaven's throne and all creation (cosmic). The trajectory is from inward praise to universal doxology. The comparison of human life to grass and flowers (vv. 15-16) — the same imagery as Isaiah 40:6-8 — is not pessimistic but liberating: because we are frail, God is gentle with us. Our smallness is not a problem to be solved but a condition that evokes divine tenderness.
preamble.friction: The superscription le-David is accepted by the text but the psalm's theology of forgiveness is remarkably mature — some scholars see it as post-exilic, reflecting the kind of theological synthesis that emerges after the exile shattered simpler frameworks of reward and punishment. The psalm's use of Exodus 34:6 is selective: it quotes the compassionate attributes but omits the warning that God 'will by no means clear the guilty' (Exodus 34:7b). This is not carelessness but theological choice — the psalmist is emphasizing one dimension of God's character for a specific pastoral purpose. The tension between justice and mercy is not resolved here; mercy is simply celebrated.
preamble.connections: Exodus 34:6-7 is the foundational text behind verse 8 — God's self-revelation as rachum ve-channun, erekh appayim ve-rav chesed ('compassionate and gracious, slow to anger and abounding in faithful love'). This creedal formula is quoted more than any other passage in the Hebrew Bible (Joel 2:13, Jonah 4:2, Nehemiah 9:17, Psalms 86:15, 145:8). The grass imagery in verses 15-16 parallels Isaiah 40:6-8 and 1 Peter 1:24-25. The fatherly compassion of verse 13 anticipates Jesus' parable of the prodigal son (Luke 15:20). The psalm's opening and closing phrase — barekhi nafshi et YHVH ('bless the LORD, O my soul') — creates a frame that also opens Psalm 104.

verses.text_hebrew: לְדָוִ֗ד בָּרְכִ֣י נַ֭פְשִׁי אֶת־יְהוָ֑ה וְכׇל־קְ֝רָבַ֗י אֶת־שֵׁ֥ם קׇדְשֽׁוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Bless the LORD, O my soul: and all that is within me, bless his holy name.
verses.rendering:
Of David.
Bless the LORD, O my soul,
and all that is within me, bless His holy name.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm begins with the psalmist commanding himself to praise. The phrase barekhi nafshi ('bless, O my soul') uses the feminine imperative of barak directed at the nephesh — the self, the life-force, the whole person. This is not an intellectual decision to praise; it is a summons to the deepest part of the self. The second line intensifies: ve-khol qeravai ('and all my inward parts') — literally 'all my innards,' the viscera, the gut. Praise must rise not from the surface of the mind but from the body's core. The object of this praise is shem qodsho ('His holy name') — the name that carries God's identity and reputation. The psalm demands total, embodied worship from the inside out.
translator_notes: The verb barak in the Piel means 'to bless, to praise, to kneel before.' When directed toward God, it means to acknowledge His greatness with gratitude. The word qeravai ('my inward parts') is anatomical — it refers to the internal organs, the seat of emotion in Hebrew thought. The psalmist wants his liver, his intestines, his kidneys — all the places where emotion is physically felt — to participate in praise.

key_terms.semantic_range: soul, self, life, breath, person, appetite, desire, the whole living being
key_terms.note: nephesh is not the Greek psyche or the Christian 'immortal soul' — it is the entire living person, the breathing self. When the psalmist says 'bless the LORD, O my nephesh,' he is summoning his whole being, not an immaterial component.

verses.text_hebrew: בָּרְכִ֣י נַ֭פְשִׁי אֶת־יְהוָ֑ה וְאַל־תִּשְׁכְּחִ֥י כׇּל־גְּמוּלָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: Bless the LORD, O my soul, and forget not all his benefits:
verses.rendering:
Bless the LORD, O my soul,
and do not forget any of His benefits.
translator_notes: The verb tishkechi ('forget') is the great danger — the soul that forgets God's acts loses the basis for praise. The word gemulav ('His benefits, His dealings') comes from gamal ('to deal with, to repay, to ripen') and includes both acts of kindness and the full weight of God's engagement with the person. The following verses (3-5) will enumerate these benefits in a catalog of divine generosity.

verses.text_hebrew: הַסֹּלֵ֥חַ לְכׇל־עֲוֺנֵ֑כִי הָ֝רֹפֵ֗א לְכׇל־תַּחֲלֻאָֽיְכִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Who forgiveth all thine iniquities; who healeth all thy diseases;
verses.rendering:
He forgives all your iniquity;
He heals all your diseases.
verses.expanded_rendering: The catalog of benefits begins with forgiveness. The participle ha-sole'ach ('the one who forgives') uses salach, a verb reserved exclusively for God in the Hebrew Bible — humans can pardon (nasa) or release (shalach), but only God performs selichah. The scope is kol avonekhi ('all your iniquity') — not some sins, not minor offenses, but the totality of moral failure. The parallel line — ha-rophe le-khol tachaluekhi ('the one who heals all your diseases') — pairs spiritual forgiveness with physical healing. In Hebrew thought, these are not separate categories. The body and the moral self are integrated; healing one often means healing the other.
translator_notes: The verb salach ('to forgive') occurs only with God as subject in the Hebrew Bible — it is a divine prerogative. This distinguishes biblical forgiveness from human pardoning. When God forgives, something happens that no human authority can accomplish.

verses.text_hebrew: הַגּוֹאֵ֣ל מִשַּׁ֣חַת חַיָּ֑יְכִי הַֽ֝מְעַטְּרֵ֗כִי חֶ֣סֶד וְרַחֲמִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Who redeemeth thy life from destruction; who crowneth thee with lovingkindness and tender mercies;
verses.rendering:
He redeems your life from the pit;
He crowns you with faithful love and compassion.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb go'el ('redeems') is the kinsman-redeemer term — the go'el is the family member obligated to buy back what was lost, to rescue the relative in danger, to restore what was forfeited. God acts as the go'el of the psalmist's life, purchasing it back from shachat ('the pit, destruction, the grave'). Then God crowns the redeemed one: ha-me'atterekhi chesed ve-rachamim ('the one who crowns you with faithful love and compassion'). The verb itter ('to crown, to surround, to adorn') means God places chesed and rachamim on the psalmist like a royal diadem. These are not abstract attributes but wearable realities — God's faithful love is placed upon the person like a crown upon a head.
translator_notes: The go'el institution (Leviticus 25, Ruth 3-4) is the legal framework here — God acts as the nearest kinsman who has both the right and the obligation to redeem. The word shachat ('pit') can mean either a physical pit, the grave, or destruction. In context, it means God rescues from death itself.

key_terms.semantic_range: loyal love, steadfast love, covenant faithfulness, kindness, mercy, devotion
key_terms.note: This is the psalm's signature word. chesed here is paired with rachamim ('compassion') as the crown God places on the redeemed person. chesed is not merely an attitude but an active force — it rescues, heals, forgives, and adorns.

key_terms.semantic_range: to redeem, to act as kinsman-redeemer, to buy back, to reclaim, to avenge
key_terms.note: The go'el is the family member who restores what was lost. When applied to God, it means God claims the psalmist as kin and exercises the obligation to rescue. The theological weight is immense: God is not a distant benefactor but a near relative who redeems.

verses.text_hebrew: הַמַּשְׂבִּ֣יעַ בַּטּ֣וֹב עֶדְיֵ֑ךְ תִּתְחַדֵּ֖שׁ כַּנֶּ֣שֶׁר נְעוּרָֽיְכִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Who satisfieth thy mouth with good things; so that thy youth is renewed like the eagle's.
verses.rendering:
He satisfies your life with good things,
so that your youth is renewed like the eagle's.
translator_notes: The verb masbia ('He satisfies') means filling to the point of completeness — no lack, no hunger, no unfulfilled desire. The word edyekh is debated: it may mean 'your ornament' (from adi, 'adornment'), 'your duration' (from ad, 'perpetuity'), or 'your mouth' (some ancient versions). We render 'your life' to capture the fullness of what God satisfies. The eagle (nesher) metaphor draws on the observation that eagles molt and regrow feathers, appearing to be renewed. The psalmist's youth (ne'urayekhi) is refreshed by God's provision — age and decay are reversed by divine generosity.

verses.text_hebrew: עֹשֵׂ֣ה צְדָק֣וֹת יְהוָ֑ה וּ֝מִשְׁפָּטִ֗ים לְכׇל־עֲשׁוּקִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD executeth righteousness and judgment for all that are oppressed.
verses.rendering:
The LORD works righteousness
and justice for all who are oppressed.
translator_notes: The plural tsedaqot ('righteous acts, acts of justice') emphasizes that God's righteousness is not a static attribute but a series of specific, concrete actions on behalf of the oppressed (ashuqim). The word ashuqim ('oppressed, crushed, exploited') refers to those ground down by more powerful people. God's justice is not impartial in the abstract sense — it is biased toward the oppressed, because they are the ones who need it.

verses.text_hebrew: יוֹדִ֣יעַ דְּרָכָ֣יו לְמֹשֶׁ֑ה לִבְנֵ֥י יִ֝שְׂרָאֵ֗ל עֲלִילוֹתָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: He made known his ways unto Moses, his acts unto the children of Israel.
verses.rendering:
He made His ways known to Moses,
His deeds to the children of Israel.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm now grounds its theology in history. God did not remain hidden; He yodia derakhav le-Mosheh ('made His ways known to Moses'). The word derakhav ('His ways') refers to God's character and mode of operating — not merely His acts but the principles behind them. Moses received both: the ways (the character) and the alilotav ('His deeds, His acts') — the specific interventions in history. The children of Israel saw the deeds; Moses understood the ways. This distinction prepares for verse 8, which quotes the specific self-revelation God gave Moses on Sinai after the golden calf disaster. The psalm's theology is not speculative but revealed — it rests on what God said about Himself.
translator_notes: The distinction between derakhav ('His ways') and alilotav ('His deeds') is important: Israel saw what God did (the plagues, the sea crossing, the manna), but Moses knew why God did it (His compassion, His faithfulness, His patience). Ways are deeper than deeds. This verse prepares for the Exodus 34:6 quotation in verse 8.

verses.text_hebrew: רַח֣וּם וְחַנּ֣וּן יְהוָ֑ה אֶ֥רֶךְ אַ֝פַּ֗יִם וְרַב־חָֽסֶד׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD is merciful and gracious, slow to anger, and plenteous in mercy.
verses.rendering:
The LORD is compassionate and gracious,
slow to anger and abounding in faithful love.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the great creedal statement — the direct quotation of Exodus 34:6, God's self-revelation to Moses on Mount Sinai. The four terms form two pairs: rachum ve-channun ('compassionate and gracious') describes God's emotional posture — He feels for His people (rachum, from rechem, 'womb') and extends undeserved favor (channun, from chen, 'grace'). The second pair — erekh appayim ve-rav chesed ('long of nostrils and great of faithful love') — describes God's behavioral pattern: He is slow to anger (literally, His nostrils are long — anger in Hebrew is a flaring of the nostrils, and God's nostrils take a very long time to flare) and overflowing with chesed. This formula is quoted more frequently than any other self-description of God in the Hebrew Bible. It is the nearest thing ancient Israel had to a creed.
translator_notes: The phrase erekh appayim ('slow to anger') is literally 'long of nostrils.' In Hebrew, anger is expressed through the nose — rapid breathing, flared nostrils. A person who is 'long of nostrils' takes a long time to get angry. Applied to God, it means His default posture is patience, not wrath.

The psalm quotes Exodus 34:6 but omits the continuation in 34:7b — 'who will by no means clear the guilty.' This selective quotation is theologically deliberate: the psalmist is celebrating mercy, not denying justice. The full character of God includes both; this psalm magnifies one.

key_terms.semantic_range: loyal love, steadfast love, covenant faithfulness, kindness, mercy, devotion
key_terms.note: In the Exodus 34:6 formula, chesed is the climactic attribute — it is what God is 'abounding in' (rav chesed). It is the operative force behind God's patience, His compassion, and His willingness to forgive.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹא־לָנֶ֥צַח יָרִ֑יב וְלֹ֖א לְעוֹלָ֣ם יִטּֽוֹר׃
verses.text_kjv: He will not always chide: neither will he keep his anger for ever.
verses.rendering:
He will not always contend,
nor will He keep His anger forever.
translator_notes: The verb yariv ('He contends, He brings a case') is legal language — God is pictured as a prosecutor who eventually drops the case. The verb yittor ('He keeps, He bears a grudge') means to maintain anger over time. The psalm's claim is radical: God's anger has an expiration date. He does not nurse grievances or hold eternal grudges. This is not a denial of divine justice but an assertion that mercy has the last word.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹ֣א כַ֭חֲטָאֵינוּ עָ֣שָׂה לָ֑נוּ וְלֹ֥א כַ֝עֲוֺנֹתֵ֗ינוּ גָּמַ֥ל עָלֵֽינוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: He hath not dealt with us after our sins; nor rewarded us according to our iniquities.
verses.rendering:
He has not dealt with us according to our sins,
nor repaid us according to our iniquities.
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse states the gap between what Israel deserves and what Israel receives. The phrase lo kachata'einu asah lanu ('not according to our sins has He dealt with us') acknowledges genuine sin (chata'einu, 'our sins') and genuine iniquity (avonoteinu, 'our crookednesses'). The psalmist is not claiming innocence. Israel sinned. The golden calf happened. The wilderness rebellions happened. But God did not respond with proportional punishment. The verb gamal ('to repay, to deal with') is the same root as gemulav ('His benefits') in verse 2 — what God actually repays is far more gracious than what the ledger would require. This is the theological center of the psalm: the disproportion between human failure and divine response.
translator_notes: The parallelism between chata'einu ('our sins,' from chata, 'to miss the mark') and avonoteinu ('our iniquities,' from avah, 'to bend, to twist, to be crooked') uses two different Hebrew words for moral failure. Sin is missing the target; iniquity is being bent out of shape. God responds to neither with what they deserve.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י כִגְבֹ֣הַּ שָׁ֭מַיִם עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ גָּבַ֥ר חַ֝סְדּ֗וֹ עַל־יְרֵאָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: For as the heaven is high above the earth, so great is his mercy toward them that fear him.
verses.rendering:
For as high as the heavens are above the earth,
so great is His faithful love toward those who fear Him.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm now measures chesed — and the unit of measurement is the distance between heaven and earth. The phrase ki-khigevohah shamayim al ha-arets ('for as the height of the heavens above the earth') establishes the maximum conceivable distance in the ancient worldview. God's chesed gavar ('prevails, is mighty, exceeds') by that same measure over those who fear Him (al yere'av). The verb gavar means 'to be strong, to prevail, to overpower' — chesed is not a gentle sentiment but a force that overwhelms. The condition — al yere'av ('over those who fear Him') — limits the promise to those who maintain reverent relationship with God. Chesed is covenantal: it operates within the relationship of awe and trust.
translator_notes: The verb gavar ('to prevail, to be mighty') applied to chesed means that faithful love is not passive — it actively prevails over obstacles, over sin, over the distance between heaven and earth. The spatial metaphor makes an abstract quality vivid: chesed is as vast as the sky.

key_terms.semantic_range: loyal love, steadfast love, covenant faithfulness, kindness, mercy, devotion
key_terms.note: Third occurrence in the psalm. Here chesed is measured against the heavens — it is cosmic in scale. The verb gavar gives it the quality of overwhelming force.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּרְחֹ֣ק מִ֭זְרָח מִֽמַּעֲרָ֑ב הִרְחִ֥יק מִ֝מֶּ֗נּוּ אֶת־פְּשָׁעֵֽינוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: As far as the east is from the west, so far hath he removed our transgressions from us.
verses.rendering:
As distant as east is from west,
so far has He removed our transgressions from us.
verses.expanded_rendering: The measurement changes from vertical (heaven above earth) to horizontal (east from west). The phrase ki-rechoq mizrach mi-ma'arav ('as the distance of east from west') is significant because unlike north and south — which have endpoints at the poles — east and west never meet. You can walk east forever and never arrive at west. The distance is infinite. God has removed (hirchiq, Hiphil of rachaq, 'to make distant') our transgressions (pesha'einu, 'our rebellions') by that infinite distance. The word pesha is the strongest term for sin in Hebrew — it means willful rebellion, deliberate transgression, not accidental failure. Even the worst category of human sin has been moved an infinite distance away from the sinner.
translator_notes: The three sin-words in the psalm — chet (v. 10, missing the mark), avon (v. 10, crookedness), and pesha (v. 12, rebellion) — cover the full spectrum of human moral failure. God addresses all three: He does not repay chet or avon proportionally (v. 10), and He removes pesha entirely (v. 12).

verses.text_hebrew: כְּרַחֵ֣ם אָ֭ב עַל־בָּנִ֑ים רִחַ֥ם יְ֝הוָ֗ה עַל־יְרֵאָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: Like as a father pitieth his children, so the LORD pitieth them that fear him.
verses.rendering:
As a father has compassion on his children,
so the LORD has compassion on those who fear Him.
verses.expanded_rendering: The metaphor shifts from spatial measurement to familial emotion. The verb rachem ('to have compassion') shares its root with rechem ('womb') — it is visceral, gut-level tenderness. The comparison is ke-rachem av al banim ('as a father has compassion on children'). The father knows his children's weakness, their immaturity, their tendency to stumble. His compassion is not ignorance of their faults but knowledge of their frailty combined with love that persists through failure. God's compassion toward those who fear Him operates on the same principle: not because they deserve it, but because they are His.
translator_notes: The father metaphor is significant in a culture where the father held absolute authority over the household. A father's compassion is not weakness but the tender side of full authority — the father who could punish chooses to show mercy. This verse anticipates the explanation in verse 14: the father knows what his children are made of.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי־ה֖וּא יָדַ֣ע יִצְרֵ֑נוּ זָ֝כ֗וּר כִּי־עָפָ֥ר אֲנָֽחְנוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: For he knoweth our frame; he remembereth that we are dust.
verses.rendering:
For He knows what we are made of;
He remembers that we are dust.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the reason for God's compassion: ki hu yada yitsrenu ('for He knows our formation, our constitution'). The word yetser ('formation, frame, what is formed') is from the verb yatsar ('to form, to fashion, to shape') — the same verb used in Genesis 2:7 when God formed the human from dust. God knows what He made us from. The second line makes it explicit: zakhur ki aphar anachnu ('He remembers that we are dust'). The word aphar ('dust, earth, soil') is the raw material of human existence — fragile, temporary, without inherent permanence. God's compassion is not despite our weakness but because of it. He does not expect from dust what He expects from angels. This verse is the theological foundation of the psalm's entire argument: God is merciful because He made us and knows what we are.
translator_notes: The word yetser will later acquire theological weight in rabbinic literature as yetser ha-ra ('the evil inclination') and yetser ha-tov ('the good inclination'). Here it simply means 'what we are formed from, our constitution.' God is a potter who knows the limitations of His clay.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱ֭נוֹשׁ כֶּחָצִ֣יר יָמָ֑יו כְּצִ֥יץ הַ֝שָּׂדֶ֗ה כֵּ֣ן יָצִֽיץ׃
verses.text_kjv: As for man, his days are as grass: as a flower of the field, so he flourisheth.
verses.rendering:
As for a human — his days are like grass;
like a flower of the field, so he blooms.
translator_notes: The word enosh ('a human, a mortal') emphasizes frailty — it is the word for humanity at its most vulnerable. The comparison to chatsir ('grass') and tsits ha-sadeh ('flower of the field') draws on the Palestinian landscape where wildflowers appear after rain and are scorched by the hot east wind (the hamsin) within days. The verb yatsits ('he blossoms') uses the same root as tsits ('flower') — the human blooms briefly and brilliantly, but the bloom does not last.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י ר֣וּחַ עָ֣בְרָה בּ֣וֹ וְאֵינֶ֑נּוּ וְלֹא־יַכִּירֶ֖נּוּ ע֣וֹד מְקוֹמֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: For the wind passeth over it, and it is gone; and the place thereof shall know it no more.
verses.rendering:
For the wind passes over it and it is gone,
and its place knows it no more.
translator_notes: The ruach ('wind, breath, spirit') that destroys the flower is the same word for the life-breath that God gives. Wind gives life and takes it away. The phrase ve-einennu ('and it is not, and he is no more') is absolute — the person simply ceases to be present. The clause ve-lo yakkivennu od meqomo ('and its place will not recognize it again') personifies the location: the very ground where the person stood will not remember them. The imagery is not bitter but factual — this is what dust does.

verses.text_hebrew: וְחֶ֤סֶד יְהוָ֨ה ׀ מֵעוֹלָ֣ם וְעַד־עוֹלָם֮ עַל־יְרֵ֫אָ֥יו וְ֝צִדְקָת֗וֹ לִבְנֵ֥י בָנִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: But the mercy of the LORD is from everlasting to everlasting upon them that fear him, and his righteousness unto children's children;
verses.rendering:
But the faithful love of the LORD is from everlasting to everlasting
upon those who fear Him,
and His righteousness to children's children,
verses.expanded_rendering: The great 'but' — ve-chesed YHVH ('but the faithful love of the LORD'). Human life is grass; God's chesed is me-olam ve-ad olam ('from everlasting to everlasting'). The phrase spans infinite time in both directions — chesed has no beginning and no end. It is the one thing in the psalm that matches God's own permanence. The grass dies; chesed does not. The condition remains: al yere'av ('upon those who fear Him'). And chesed extends generationally: ve-tsidqato livnei vanim ('and His righteousness to children's children'). God's faithful love outlasts the individual and covers the descendants. This is the psalm's answer to human fragility: we are dust, but we are dust loved by an everlasting love.
translator_notes: The fourth and climactic use of chesed in the psalm. The phrase me-olam ve-ad olam is the strongest temporal expression in Hebrew — it means 'from perpetuity to perpetuity.' Chesed is set on the same timescale as God Himself.

key_terms.semantic_range: loyal love, steadfast love, covenant faithfulness, kindness, mercy, devotion
key_terms.note: Fourth and final occurrence. Here chesed receives its ultimate description: it spans eternity. While humans are grass and flowers, chesed is from everlasting to everlasting — the permanent quality of the permanent God directed toward impermanent creatures.

verses.text_hebrew: לְשֹׁמְרֵ֥י בְרִית֑וֹ וּלְזֹכְרֵ֥י פִ֝קֻּדָ֗יו לַעֲשׂוֹתָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: To such as keep his covenant, and to those that remember his commandments to do them.
verses.rendering:
to those who keep His covenant
and remember His precepts to do them.
translator_notes: This verse qualifies the promise of verse 17. The chesed that spans eternity is directed toward those who shomerei verito ('keep His covenant') and zokherei piqqudav ('remember His precepts'). The verb zakar ('to remember') paired with la'asotam ('to do them') means that remembering is not intellectual but active — you remember the commandments by performing them. The covenant framework is explicit: chesed operates within covenant relationship.

verses.text_hebrew: יְֽהוָ֗ה בַּ֭שָּׁמַיִם הֵכִ֣ין כִּסְא֑וֹ וּ֝מַלְכוּת֗וֹ בַּכֹּ֥ל מָשָֽׁלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD hath prepared his throne in the heavens; and his kingdom ruleth over all.
verses.rendering:
The LORD has established His throne in the heavens,
and His kingdom rules over all.
translator_notes: The verb hekhin ('He has established, He has prepared') uses kun ('to be firm, stable') in the Hiphil — God's throne is not temporary furniture but a permanent fixture of the cosmic architecture. The phrase malkhuto bakkol mashalah ('His kingdom over everything rules') asserts absolute sovereignty. The scope is universal: bakkol means 'over everything' — not just Israel, not just the earth, but the entire created order. This verse provides the theological basis for the cosmic praise that follows.

verses.text_hebrew: בָּרְכ֤וּ יְהוָ֨ה ׀ מַלְאָכָ֗יו גִּבֹּ֣רֵי כֹ֭חַ עֹשֵׂ֣י דְבָר֑וֹ לִ֝שְׁמֹ֗עַ בְּק֣וֹל דְּבָרֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Bless the LORD, ye his angels, that excel in strength, that do his commandments, hearkening unto the voice of his word.
verses.rendering:
Bless the LORD, you His angels,
you mighty ones who do His word,
obeying the voice of His command.
translator_notes: The call to praise expands from the psalmist's soul to the angelic realm. The mal'akhav ('His angels, His messengers') are giborei koach ('mighty ones of power') — beings of immense strength. Yet their power is exercised in obedience: osei devaro ('doers of His word'), lishmo'a be-qol devaro ('listening to the voice of His word'). Angelic might is defined by submission to God's commands. The strongest beings in the universe are characterized by their obedience.

verses.text_hebrew: בָּרְכ֣וּ יְ֭הוָה כׇּל־צְבָאָ֑יו מְ֝שָׁרְתָ֗יו עֹשֵׂ֥י רְצוֹנֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Bless ye the LORD, all ye his hosts; ye ministers of his, that do his pleasure.
verses.rendering:
Bless the LORD, all His hosts,
His ministers who do His will.
translator_notes: The tseva'av ('His hosts, His armies') expands from individual angels to the entire heavenly army. The mesharetav ('His ministers') uses sharat, the verb for priestly or courtly service — the same word used in Psalm 101:6 for those who serve the king. The phrase osei retsono ('doers of His will, His pleasure') defines service as alignment with God's desire. The heavenly hierarchy is summoned to praise.

verses.text_hebrew: בָּרְכ֤וּ יְהוָ֨ה ׀ כׇּֽל־מַעֲשָׂ֗יו בְּכׇל־מְקֹמ֥וֹת מֶמְשַׁלְתּ֑וֹ בָּרְכִ֥י נַ֝פְשִׁ֗י אֶת־יְהוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Bless the LORD, all his works in all places of his dominion: bless the LORD, O my soul.
verses.rendering:
Bless the LORD, all His works,
in every place of His dominion.
Bless the LORD, O my soul.
verses.expanded_rendering: The final verse brings the expanding circles to their widest circumference and then contracts to the starting point. First: kol ma'asav ('all His works') — everything God has made, animate and inanimate, is summoned to praise. The scope: be-khol meqomot memshalto ('in every place of His dominion') — since His kingdom rules over all (v. 19), this means everywhere. The entire cosmos is a choir. Then the psalm returns to where it began: barekhi nafshi et YHVH ('bless the LORD, O my soul'). The frame closes. The individual soul that started the psalm now joins the universal chorus. The psalmist is one voice in a cosmic symphony of praise, and yet it is his voice — his nephesh, his gut, his inward parts — that both opens and closes the hymn. The personal and the cosmic are not opposites; they are concentric circles with the same center.
translator_notes: The return to barekhi nafshi et YHVH creates an inclusio — the literary device of beginning and ending with the same phrase. The psalm has traveled from the individual soul through Israel's history, through God's cosmic character, through heaven's angels, through all creation, and back to the single soul. The message is clear: your private praise participates in the praise of the universe.
